coredata.dc:title: Sulfur quantum dots: A novel fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ and phytic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Zeming
Gao, Yin
Huang, Zhengyu
Chen, Donglin
Sun, Jiahui
Zhou, Li
coredata.dc:description: Exploring an effective sensor to detect Fe3+ and phytic acid (PA) is of great importance because the levels of Fe3+ and PA have a vital impact on human health. Herein, we report a metal-free fluorescent nanoprobe for sensitive and selective sensing of Fe3+ and PA based on the sulfur quantum dots (SQDs). The SQDs with high fluorescence quantum yield (17.6%) and low cytotoxicity can be directly synthesized from cheap elemental sulfur by one-pot reaction. The SQDs display responsive and selective fluorescence “on–off-on” behavior towards Fe3+ and PA based on the introduction and elimination of electron transfer effect. The addition of Fe3+ can cause non-radiative electron transfer associated with fluorescence quenching of SQDs, while the addition of PA enables SQDs/Fe3+ complex release Fe3+ to recover the fluorescence of SQDS. By utilizing these SQDs as a fluorescent nanoprobe, it shows good linear relationship for Fe3+ and PA in the range of 0.05–120 µM and 0–12 µM, respectively. Moreover, its limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ and PA can reach as low as 102 and 73.5 nM, respectively, which compares favorably to many reported fluorescent probes. Additionally, the use of this SQDs-based sensor to detect PA in fresh carrot and lettuce samples was examined with satisfactory performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur quantum dots
Fluorescence detection
Fe3+
Phytic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of PA in vegetable samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Open Research Fund of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magnetochemical Functional Materials
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51663007 ), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2017GXNSFFA198002 ), Bagui Scholar Program of Guangxi Province , and the Open Research Fund of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magnetochemical Functional Materials .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of SQDs. Photographs of aqueous dispersion of SQDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right). (b) TEM and high-resolution TEM (inset) images of SQDs. (c) Size distribution histogram of SQDs (100 particles were measured). (d) FTIR spectra of SQDs and pure PEG-200. (e) Raman spectra of SQDs and sublimed sulfur. XPS survey (f) and S 2p (g) spectra of SQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption (Abs), photoluminescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of SQDs in water. (b) Emission spectra of aqueous dispersion of SQDs with different excitation wavelengths. (c) Emission spectra of SQDs after adding different concentrations of Fe3+ (λex = 350 nm). (d) I/I0 value of SQDs against Fe3+ in the range of 0.05–120 μM. (e) I/I0 value of SQDs against 110 μM of diverse metal ions. (f) Photographs of aqueous dispersion of SQDs after mixing 150 μM of diverse metal ions under 365 nm UV light.

(a) Emission spectra of the SQDs/Fe3+ system in water after adding different concentrations of PA (λex = 350 nm). (b) I/I0 value of SQDs/Fe3+ system against PA in the range of 0–12 μM. (c) Emission spectra of SQDs before and after adding 20 μM of PA (λex = 350 nm). (d) Effects of contacting time on I/I0 value of SQDs/Fe3+ system. The concentration of PA is 12 µM. (e) I/I0 value of SQDs/Fe3+ system against 12 µM of diverse molecules and metal ions. (f) I/I0 value of SQDs/Fe3+ system against 12 μM of PA (red column) and 12 μM of other substance together with 12 μM of PA (blue columns). I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity of SQDs/Fe3+ system in the absence and presence of PA (or other substance), respectively.

Zeta potential values (a) and time-resolved fluorescence-decay curves (b) of SQDs, SQDs/Fe3+ system before and after mixing PA. (c) Schematic illustration of the fluorescence detection mechanism for Fe3+ and PA based on SQDs probe.
Comparison of diverse fluorescent probes for sensing of Fe3+ and PA.
Monitoring of PA in fresh vegetable samples (n = 5).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The concentration of PA was determined by fluorescence detection method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The concentration of PA was determined by titration method according to the literature [11].

head.ce:title: Sulfur quantum dots: A novel fluorescent probe for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ and phytic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal and Materials (Ministry of Education), and College of Materials Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal and Materials (Ministry of Education), and College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal and Materials (Ministry of Education), and College of Materials Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring an effective sensor to detect Fe3+ and phytic acid (PA) is of great importance because the levels of Fe3+ and PA have a vital impact on human health. Herein, we report a metal-free fluorescent nanoprobe for sensitive and selective sensing of Fe3+ and PA based on the sulfur quantum dots (SQDs). The SQDs with high fluorescence quantum yield (17.6%) and low cytotoxicity can be directly synthesized from cheap elemental sulfur by one-pot reaction. The SQDs display responsive and selective fluorescence “on–off-on” behavior towards Fe3+ and PA based on the introduction and elimination of electron transfer effect. The addition of Fe3+ can cause non-radiative electron transfer associated with fluorescence quenching of SQDs, while the addition of PA enables SQDs/Fe3+ complex release Fe3+ to recover the fluorescence of SQDS. By utilizing these SQDs as a fluorescent nanoprobe, it shows good linear relationship for Fe3+ and PA in the range of 0.05–120 µM and 0–12 µM, respectively. Moreover, its limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ and PA can reach as low as 102 and 73.5 nM, respectively, which compares favorably to many reported fluorescent probes. Additionally, the use of this SQDs-based sensor to detect PA in fresh carrot and lettuce samples was examined with satisfactory performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron ion (Fe3+), which plays important role in physiological process such as cellular metabolism, oxygen carry and enzyme catalysis, is one of the most essential metal ions for living systems [1–3]. Abnormal Fe3+ level may result in serious adverse effect on human health (e.g., iron deficiency anemia and heart disease). Phytic acid (PA), not only exists in a variety of plant seeds, fruits and vegetables as the main storage form of phosphorus, but also has a positive effect on animals and human beings. Very recently, PA has been found to possess the potential to reduce cholesterol risk and prevent cancer [4,5]. In order to better understand the role of Fe3+ and PA in physiological processes, it is necessary to explore effective and applicable detection techniques for Fe3+ and PA [6–8]. Numerous analytic techniques, including inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy, high-performance liquid chromatography, titration, electrochemical analysis, Raman spectroscopy and fluorometric method, have been developed to quantify Fe3+ and PA [9–14]. Among them, particular attention has been paid on the fluorometric technique due to its advantages of ease of operation, fast detection rate, and high sensitivity.

Until now, several kinds of fluorescent materials including small fluorophores, conjugated polymers, and metal nanoclusters [15–19], have been synthesized and utilized as a probe to monitor Fe3+ and PA. Unfortunately, most of them suffer limitations, such as complicated synthesis associated with high cost, poor photostability, and high toxicity, all of which seriously impede their practical sensing applications. In recent years, the utilization of metal-free fluorescent quantum dots (e.g., carbon dots and silicon dots) to determine Fe3+ and PA has received special attention because of their unique optical properties and low toxicity [20–22]. However, the sensitivity and selectivity of these fluorescent nanodots still need to be improved. Sulfur quantum dots (SQDs), as a novel metal-free fluorescent nanomaterial, have gained tremendous attention in the past three years due to their unique fluorescent property and low toxicity [23]. Meanwhile, the SQDs can be directly and readily synthesized from abundant and low-cost elemental sulfur [24–26]. At present, the SQDs have posed great potential in the fields of optical devices, cellular imaging, catalysis and sensor [27–32]. Although SQDs have been employed as a fluorescent nanoprobe to sense metal ions and organic molecules, as far as we know, the investigation of SQDs-based assay is still in its infancy.

In the present work, we report the simple synthesis of fluorescent SQDs for sensitive and selective sensing of Fe3+ and PA based on the fluorescence “on–off-on” behavior of SQDs. Using low molecular weight polyethylene glycol (PEG-200) as ligand, highly fluorescent SQDs with multiple surface hydroxyl groups could be readily synthesized by one-pot reaction between elemental sulfur and NaOH under O2 atmosphere. The resulted SQDs displayed responsive fluorescence quenching behavior towards Fe3+ due to the existence of electron transfer effect. Moreover, the quenched fluorescence could be efficiently recovered by addition of PA because the PA can capture Fe3+ from the SQDs/ Fe3+ system based on its strong complexation interaction with Fe3+. According to this fluorescence “on–off-on” phenomenon, the use of SQDs as a fluorescent sensor for determination of Fe3+ and PA was investigated systematically. In addition, we also demonstrate the feasibility of this SQDs-based probe in practical sensing by performing PA detection with vegetable samples.

Sublimed sulfur powder, NaOH, polyethylene glycol (PEG-200, the number average molecular weight is 200 Da), phytic acid (PA), FeCl3·6H2O (Fe3+), FeSO4·7H2O (Fe2+), CuCl2·2H2O (Cu2+), Pb(CH3COO)2·3H2O (Pb2+), Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (Ni2+), ZnNO3·2H2O (Zn2+), CaCl2 (Ca2+), Co(NO3)2·6H2O (Co2+), KCl (K+), LiCl (Li+), NaCl (Na+), MnCl2·4H2O (Mn2+), CdCl2 (Cd2+), MgSO4 (Mg2+), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), arginine (Arg), cysteine (Cys), histidine (His), glucose (Glu), oxalic acid (Oa), vitamin B1 (VB1), urea, and starch (Sta) were supplied by Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and used as received.

The SQDs were synthesized based on our previous reports with minor modification [26]. Briefly, PEG-200 (3 mL), NaOH (4.2 g) and sublimed sulfur powder (1.35 g) were added into a round-bottom flask containing 55 mL of deionized water. The mixture was stirred at 30 °C for 0.5 h to form a homogenous solution and then was agitated at 90 °C for 10 h under O2 atmosphere. After natural cooling to room temperature, the resulting SQDs solution was purified by dialysis against water (Mw cutoff = 500 Da) for 24 h and followed by filtration using a Millipore membrane (0.22 μm).

In a typical sensing procedure, 60 μL of aqueous dispersion of SQDs (1.8 mg/mL) was added into a quartz cuvette. Then, Fe3+ solution (or other metal ions) with known volume and concentration was added into the quartz cuvette. The resulting mixture was adjusted to 3 mL with water. After shaking for 1 min, the fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were recorded with excitation wavelength at 350 nm.

The fluorescence detection of PA was conducted using SQDs/Fe3+ system as a “off–on” probe. Typically, 60 μL of SQDs dispersion and 300 μL of Fe3+ (3 mM) were incubated for 1 min in a quartz cuvette. Subsequently, 30 μL of PA solution with known concentration (0.1–2 mM) was added and the mixture was adjusted to 3 mL with deionized water. After shaking for 6 min, the emission spectra of mixtures were measured under excitation at 350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of PA in vegetable samples
ce:section.ce:para: Lettuce and carrot, obtained from a supermarket (Guilin, China), were chosen as representative vegetable samples. In a typical procedure for sample pretreatment, 10 g of lettuce was crushed in a grinder to afford a homogeneous juice. After that, the obtained juice was added into deionized water and the volume of mixture was adjusted to 200 mL. After agitation at room temperature for 1 h, the mixture was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min. Subsequently, the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to afford lettuce solution. The following sensing procedure was similar to that in deionized water. Briefly, 60 μL of SQDs dispersion and 300 μL of Fe3+ (3 mM) were mixed at room temperature for 1 min before adding 30 μL of PA solution (0.2, 0.4, and 0.6 mM). After adjusting the volume of mixture to 3 mL with the obtained vegetable solution, the mixture was incubated for 5 min. Finally, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture at 436 nm was determined using the excitation wavelength at 350 nm.

As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, fluorescent SQDs could be straightforwardly synthesized from sublimed sulfur and NaOH by employing PEG-200 as ligand under pure O2 condition. The sublimed sulfur first reacted with NaOH to form sodium polysulfide, and then the polysulfide ions were reduced into zero valent sulfur by O2. Our previous studies have demonstrated that performing the reaction under O2 condition can significantly accelerate the reaction rate, enhance the fluorescent intensity of SQDs and improve the yield of SQDs [18,26]. Notably, even under the same reaction conditions, no fluorescent product was obtained if PEG-200 or sublimed sulfur was not added. Herein, we intentionally selected the low molecular weight PEG-200 as ligand, because compared with high molecular weight PEG, the PEG-200 may endow the SQDs with more hydroxyl groups. The resulted SQDs could be homogeneously dispersed in water without noticeable sedimentation even after storage for two months. Under UV illumination, bright cyan fluorescence was observed from the aqueous dispersion of SQDs (Fig. 1a).

The morphology and size of SQDs were determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The resultant SQDs show uniformly quasi-spherical shape and their average diameter was calculated to be around 3.0 nm (Fig. 1b,c). The corresponding high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 1b) shows clear lattice fringes of 0.213 nm, which is consistent with the previously reported SQDs [26,29,33]. Meanwhile, the structure of SQDs was analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Fig. S1). The diffraction peak positions of SQDs matched well with the sulfur polycrystalline phase (JCPDS Card No. 83-2285) [29]. As illustrated in Fig. 1d, the SQDs show very similar Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum to that of pure PEG-200, indicating the existence of PEG-200 ligand on the surface of SQDs. The hydrophilic PEG-200 ligand can endow the SQDs with superior aqueous dispersibility and stability. The absorption band centered at around 3400 cm−1 confirms the presence of multiple surface hydroxyl groups in the SQDs. Additionally, the characteristic peaks presented in the Raman spectrum of SQDs matched well with the sublimated sulfur sample, revealed that the obtained fluorescent nanodots were SQDs (Fig. 1e). Furthermore, the surface state of SQDs was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1f) indicates that the SQDs sample contains C, O, and S elements, agreeing well with the composition of SQDs. The corresponding high-resolution XPS S 2p spectrum (Fig. 1g) reveals that the SQDs comprise three kinds of sulfur, including SO3 2- (167.4 eV), SO4 2- (168.2 eV), and zero-valent sulfur (163.4 and 164.5 eV) [24,26]. The existence of oxidized sulfur may be caused by the oxidation of surface zero-valent sulfur during the reaction and purification processes. The elemental analysis (EA) result (Table S1) shows that the content of S, C, H and O elements in SQDs is 37.25 wt%, 24.18 wt%, 6.23 wt%, and 32.34 wt% (calculated), respectively.

The cytotoxicity of SQDs was examined based on thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay and the results demonstrate the low cytotoxicity of SQDs. As presented in Fig. S2, even incubation with 100 µg/mL of SQDs, the cell viability of HeLa cells is still higher than 86%. Hence, the obtained SQDs possess low toxicity, enabling them promising for practical fluorescence detection application.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of SQDs dispersion (Fig. 2 a) presents two obvious bands centered at 214 and 264 nm, which are caused by the n-σ* and n-π* transition of sulfur atoms, respectively [24–26]. Like most reported SQDs, herein, the obtained SQDs also exhibit excitation-dependent emission behavior (Fig. 2b). By varying the excitation wavelength from 280 to 440 nm, the corresponding emission peak shifts from 428 to 517 nm, respectively. The maximum emission peak locates at around 436 nm under excitation of 350 nm (Fig. 2b), which is in agreement with the excitation spectrum (Fig. 2a). The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of SQDs was measured to be 17.6%, higher than many reported SQDs [24,29,30,31]. Considering that the practical detection condition is complicated, the fluorescence stability of SQDs was evaluated by varying the solution pH and adding different concentrations of NaCl electrolyte. Unlike some fluorescent nanomaterials with susceptible fluorescence [33,34], the obtained SQDs exhibit stable fluorescence against pH change in the range of of 4–11 and high concentration of NaCl (e.g., 1.0 M) (Fig. S3). In addition, the photostabiltiy of SQDs was assessed by continues illumination with a xenon lamp (5 W). For comparison, commercially available fluorescein was also illuminated under the same condition. After illumination for 10 min, the fluorescent intensity of SQDs and fluorescein decreased by 1% and 4.4% (Fig. S4), respectively, suggesting that the SQDs possess favorable photostability.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of SQDs for Fe3+, the effects of other metal ions on the fluorescence of SQDs were investigated. At relatively high concentration (e.g., 110 μM), the quenching effect of other metal ions is slight (e.g., Cu2+, Fe2+ and Pb2+) or negligible (e.g., Mg2+. Cd2+ and Mn2+) as compared with that of Fe3+ (Fig. 2e). At low concentration (10–50 μM), the quenching effect of Cu2+, Fe2+ and Pb2+ became very slight (Fig. S6), while the Fe3+ still exhibited high fluorescence quenching capability. As shown in Fig. 2f, after mixing 150 μM of different metal ions, the SQDs dispersions could still emit obvious fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp except the sample with Fe3+. Moreover, the coexistence of other metal ions also has negligible influence on the fluorescence detection of Fe3+ (Fig. S7). Based on the above results, it is believed that the SQDs have a good application prospect in rapid, sensitive and highly selective detection of Fe3+.

Since the PA molecule possesses multiple negatively charged phosphate groups (Fig. S8), it possesses very strong affinity for Fe3+. It is expected that the PA is able to capture Fe3+ from the SQDs/Fe3+ system, and thus recovering the quenched fluorescence of SQDs. To verify this conjecture, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture of SQDs and Fe3+ (300 µM) after gradual addition of PA were recorded. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity of SQDs/Fe3+ system gradually enhances with increasing the adding amount of PA. When the concentration of PA reaches 15 μM, the fluorescence intensity increased by 3.4 times as compared with the SQDs/Fe3+ system without PA. Additionally, the relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) displays an excellent linear relationship (R2 = 0.997) towards PA in the range of 0–12 μM (Fig. 3b). The LOD was calculated to be as low as 73.5 nM, which compares favorably to numerous reported fluorescent sensors [7,8,18,19,21] (see Table 1). When PA is directly added to the SQDs dispersion, even if the concentration of PA reaches 20 μM, no obvious change of the fluorescence intensity of SQDs is observed (Fig. 3c). Hence, the above fluorescence recovery behavior is caused by the release of Fe3+ quencher from the SQDs/Fe3+ system since the PA possesses very strong affinity towards Fe3+. It is noted that the fluorescence recover process is fast and could reach equilibrium within 5 min (Fig. 3d). In addition, the sensing selectivity for PA was also evaluated by adding other common molecules and metal ions in plant, including glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), arginine (Arg), cysteine (Cys), histidine (His), glucose (Glu), starch (Sta), oxalic acid (Oa), metal ions, Vitamin B1 (VB1) and urea into the SQDs/Fe3+ system. As illustrated in Fig. 3e, they have no obvious effect on the fluorescence intensity of SQDs/Fe3+ system. Moreover, even if the above substances coexist with PA, their effect on the fluorescence detection of PA by SQDs/Fe3+ system is still slight or negligible (Fig. 3f).Thus, the SQDs/Fe3+ system hold potential for serving as an anti-interference fluorescent platform for selective sensing of PA.

We next applied the SQD/Fe3+ system to monitor PA in fresh carrot and lettuce. Based on the linear calibration curve presented in Fig. 3b, the contents of PA in carrot and lettuce were measured to be 5.26 and 0.03 mg/g, which is in accordance with the reported value [41]. Then, a recovery study was performed on the vegetable samples spiked with standard PA solutions (2, 4 and 6 μM). For comparison, the concentration of PA was also determined by titration method according to the literature [11]. As shown in Table 2 , the quantitative spike recoveries for detection of PA in carrot and lettuce samples are in the range of 95.3–103.3% with a relative standard deviation (RSD) value of less than 3.5%. In addition, the concentration of PA determined by fluorescence method was comparable to that of titration method. Nevertheless, the fluorescence detection process is simple and fast. On the basis of these results, it is reliable and feasible to employ SQDs/Fe3+ system as a fluorescence “off–on” switch for detection of PA in real samples.

To probe the sensing mechanism, the zeta potential values of SQDs before and after incubation with Fe3+ and PA were monitored. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, the zeta potential of SQDs changed from −15.36 to 19.23 mV with introduction of Fe3+, implying that the SQDs can interact with Fe3+ to form complex via their surface hydroxyl groups. When PA was added into the SQDs/Fe3+ system, the zeta potential value decreased to −11.94 mV again. This is because the addition of PA can release the Fe3+ from the SQDs/Fe3+ complex since the phosphate groups of PA have much stronger affinity for Fe3+ than those of hydroxyl groups in SQDs. Moreover, the influences of Fe3+ and PA on the fluorescence lifetime of SQDs were also studied. As shown in Fig. 4b, with the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence lifetime of SQDs decreased from 3.02 to 2.58 ns, and then with the addition of PA, the fluorescence lifetime of SQDs returned to 2.93 ns again. Based on the above results, the possible sensing mechanism of SQDs towards Fe3+ and PA is deduced. Firstly, the Fe3+-induced fluorescence quenching of SQDs is due to the presence of electron transfer from SQDs to Fe3+, as confirmed by the obvious decline of fluorescence lifetime of SQDs after adding Fe3+ (Fig. 4b). When the SQDs are excited, the electrons of SQDs rise from the ground state (S0) to the excited state (S1) (Fig. 4c). Subsequently, the electrons in the excited state may enter the half-full 3d orbital of Fe3+, results in non-radiative electron transfer associated with fluorescence quenching of SQDs [31,38]. Since the affinity of PA to Fe3+ is much stronger than that of SQDs, the addition of PA enables SQDs/Fe3+ complex release Fe3+, as demonstrated by the results of zeta potential and fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 4a,b). Thus, the electron transfer process can be eliminated, leading to recover of the fluorescence of SQDS (Fig. 4c).

In conclusion, we have developed an effective fluorescent nanoprobe for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ and PA based on the fluorescence “on–off–on” behavior of SQDs. The SQDs with multiple hydroxyl groups showed superior water dispersibility, strong and tunable emission, and low cytotoxicity. The fluorescence of SQDs can be selectively quenched by Fe3+ due to the formation of SQDs/Fe3+ complex associated with electron transfer effect. Because the PA can efficiently capture Fe3+ from the SQDs/Fe3+ complex to eliminate the quenching effect, the SQDs/Fe3+ system holds great promise for PA detection based on the fluorescence “off–on” behavior induced by PA. Moreover, this SQDs-based probe has been successfully employed to determine PA in practical carrot and lettuce samples with satisfactory performance.

Zeming Huang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Yin Gao: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Zhengyu Huang: Methodology, Investigation. Donglin Chen: Investigation. Jiahui Sun: Investigation, Validation. Li Zhou: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51663007), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2017GXNSFFA198002), Bagui Scholar Program of Guangxi Province, and the Open Research Fund of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical and Magnetochemical Functional Materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106656.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile green and one-pot synthesis of seville orange derived carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ ions
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.M. Senol, E. Bozkurt, Facile green and one-pot synthesis of seville orange derived carbon dots as a fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ ions, Microchem. J. 159 (2020) 105357.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green preparation of versatile nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from watermelon juice for cell imaging, detection of Fe3+ ions and cysteine, and optical thermometry
J. Mol. Liq.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Lu, Y. Duan, Y. Song, J. Tan, L. Zhou, Green preparation of versatile nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from watermelon juice for cell imaging, detection of Fe3+ ions and cysteine, and optical thermometry, J. Mol. Liq. 269 (2018) 766–774.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic liquid-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iron ion and ascorbic acid
Colloids Sur. A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Xie, X. Sun, J. Jiao, S. Xin, Ionic liquid-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iron ion and ascorbic acid, Colloids Sur. A 529 (2017) 38–44.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Methods of detecting plant raw materials in meat products-a review
Czech J. Food Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Vanha, F. Kvasnicka, Methods of detecting plant raw materials in meat products-a review, Czech J. Food Sci. 29 (2011) 299–307.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analytical methods for determination of phytic acid and other inositol phosphates: a review
Molecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Marolt, M. Kolar, Analytical methods for determination of phytic acid and other inositol phosphates: a review, Molecules 26 (2021) 174–202.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hyaluronic acid as a material for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots and its application for selective detection of Fe3+ ion and folic acid
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Wen, Z. Pu, Y. Yang, J. Wang, B. Wu, Y. Hu, P. Liu, J. Ling, Q. Cao, Hyaluronic acid as a material for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots and its application for selective detection of Fe3+ ion and folic acid,Microchem. J. 159 (2020) 105364.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synchronous fluorescence determination of phytic acid in foodstuffs and urine based on replacement reaction
Phytochem. Anal.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Cao, N. Dong, J. Chen, Synchronous fluorescence determination of phytic acid in foodstuffs and urine based on replacement reaction, Phytochem. Anal. 22 (2011) 119–123.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel fluorimetric sensing strategy for highly sensitive detection of phytic acid and hydrogen peroxide
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Qu, W. Na, Y. Nie, X. Su, A novel fluorimetric sensing strategy for highly sensitive detection of phytic acid and hydrogen peroxide, Anal. Chim. Acta 1039 (2018) 74–81.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Anion-exchange high-performance liquid chromatography with conductivity detection for the analysis of phytic acid in food
J. Chromatogr. A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Talamond, S. Doulbeau, I. Rochette, J.P. Guyot, S. Treche, Anion-exchange high-performance liquid chromatography with conductivity detection for the analysis of phytic acid in food, J. Chromatogr. A 871 (2000) 7–12.
